content.text:
Now I can clearly see the bug. On line 12, the code creates a `new RegExp(x.text[0])` — it takes the first element of the `text` array of a header and uses it as a regex pattern. If the text contains special regex characters like parentheses, it will cause an invalid regex error. 

The error message mentions `/` being included as well, which means the text content contains forward slashes or parentheses (like `(`). The fix should escape the special regex characters before constructing the regex.

Let me also look at the HTML parsing to understand what `x.text` contains, and check if there are existing

file.content:
'use strict';
/*jshint asi: true */

var test = require('tap').test
  , transform = require('../lib/transform')

test('\ngiven a file that includes html with header tags and maxHeaderLevel 8', function (t) {
  var content = require('fs').readFileSync(__dirname + '/fixtures/readme-with-html.md', 'utf8');
  var headers = transform(content, 'github.com', 8);

  t.same(
      headers.toc.split('\n')
    , [ '**Table of Contents**  *generated with [DocToc](https://github.com/thlorenz/doctoc)*',
        '',
        '- [Installation](#installation)',
        '- [API](#api)',
        '    - [dockops::Containers(docker) → {Object}](#dockopscontainersdocker-%E2%86%92-object)',
        '      - [Parameters:](#parameters)',
        '      - [Returns:](#returns)',
        '    - [dockops::Containers::activePorts(cb)](#dockopscontainersactiveportscb)',
        '      - [Parameters:](#parameters-1)',
        '    - [dockops::Containers::clean(id, cb)](#dockopscontainerscleanid-cb)',
        '      - [Parameters:](#parameters-2)',
        '- [License](#license)',
        '' ]
    , 'generates correct toc for non html and html headers'
  )

  t.end()
})

test('\ngiven a file that includes html with header tags using default maxHeaderLevel', function (t) {
  var content = require('fs').readFileSync(__dirname + '/fixtures/readme-with-html.md', 'utf8');
  var headers = transform(content);

  t.same(
      headers.toc.split('\n')
    , [ '**Table of Contents**  *generated with [DocToc](https://github.com/thlorenz/doctoc)*',
        '',
        '- [Installation](#installation)',
        '- [API](#api)',
        '    - [dockops::Containers(docker) → {Object}](#dockopscontainersdocker-%E2%86%92-object)',
        '    - [dockops::Containers::activePorts(cb)](#dockopscontainersactiveportscb)',
        '    - [dockops::Containers::clean(id, cb)](#dockopscontainerscleanid-cb)',
        '- [License](#license)',
        '' ]
    , 'generates correct toc for non html and html headers omitting headers larger than maxHeaderLevel'
  )
  t.end()
})

test('\ngiven a file with headers embedded in code', function (t) {
  var content = require('fs').readFileSync(__dirname + '/fixtures/readme-with-code.md', 'utf8');
  var headers = transform(content);

  t.same(
      headers.toc.split('\n')
    , [ '## Table of Contents',
        '',
        '- [Single Backticks](#single-backticks)',
        '- [Multiple Backticks](#multiple-backticks)',
        '- [code tag](#code-tag)',
        '- [pre tag](#pre-tag)',
        '' ]
    , 'generates a correct toc when headers are embedded in code blocks'
  )

  t.end()
})

test('\ngiven a file with benign backticks', function (t) {
  var content = require('fs').readFileSync(__dirname + '/fixtures/readme-benign-backticks.md', 'utf8');
  var headers = transform(content);

  t.same(
      headers.toc.split('\n')
    , [ '**Table of Contents**  *generated with [DocToc](https://github.com/thlorenz/doctoc)*',
        '',
        '- [Hello, world!](#hello-world)',
        '- [Add this header](#add-this-header)',
        '- [And also this one](#and-also-this-one)',
        '' ]
    , 'generates a correct toc when readme has benign backticks'
  )

  t.end()
})

Hmm, the test passes. Let me look more carefully at how the code flows. The issue is in `addLinenos` at line 12. Let me trace through more carefully what `x.text[0]` contains. The bug description says the README text is: ``` This readme causes an error `

` text `

`) ``` But the error message shows the regex trying to match text like "` element tells the rendering program... " — the backtick is significant. Let me figure out what exactly gets parsed. Let me try using doctoc directly

content.text:
Interesting, it doesn't error on this. Let me re-read the error more carefully. The error is from `/usr/local/lib/node_modules/doctoc/...` which suggests a globally installed version — maybe a different version. Let me look more closely at what happens. The error shows: ``` SyntaxError: Invalid regular expression: /` element tells the rendering program that the content inside is a form. ``` This text seems to be from an HTML tutorial or reference about the `

` tag. The `x.text[0]` likely contains text like: ``` ` element tells the rendering program... ``` or simply starts with `` ` ``. But the key issue is that `x.text[0]` could contain ANY regex-special characters. Let me look at this from a different angle — let me check if there are special characters like `+` or `*` or `(` in header text that would cause issues:

input.description: Test HTML parsing to understand text content

content.content:
opening h1
text: "a+b"
closing h1

input.description: Test regex with special character +

content.content: false

content.text: So `a+b` works as a regex (just matches differently — it matches `ab` not `a+b`). Let me try with parentheses

input.description: Test regex with parentheses - should fail

content.content: false

content.text: Hmm, `a(b)` works but matches `ab`. Let me try an unterminated group like in the error

input.description: Test regex with unterminated group

[eval]:1
console.log(new RegExp('a(b').test('a(b'))
            ^

    at new RegExp (<anonymous>)
    at [eval]:1:13
    at runScriptInThisContext (node:internal/vm:209:10)

content.text:
There we go! An unterminated group like `a(b` causes the error. The key insight is that `decodeEntities: true` decodes HTML entities, so text that contains `(` or other regex-special characters can cause this. Now, the real question is: when would `x.text[0]` contain something like `a(b`? Let me trace through the actual doctoc flow more carefully. Let me look at the exact scenario from the bug report. The README has: ``` This readme causes an error `

` text `

` more text (`

`) ``` The `

` tag gets parsed. But what text is inside it? Let me check

input.description: Analyze how markdown parses the bug report README

content.content: HTML blocks: []

input.description: Analyze how markdown parses basic HTML

content.text:
OK so the backtick-quoted HTML tags in the original bug report README are inline code in markdown, so they won't be parsed as HTML. The actual problem in the bug report might be a different version where the `<h1>` etc. are real HTML tags.

Regardless, the core issue is clear: `new RegExp(x.text[0])` on line 12 of `get-html-headers.js` does not escape special regex characters. If any HTML header contains text with characters like `(`, `[`, `{`, `+`, `*`, `?`, `.`, `\`, `|`, `^`, `$`, etc., the regex could either fail or produce incorrect matches.

The fix is to escape special regex characters. Let me also look more carefully at the `addLinenos` function — the `x.text` is an array (populated by `text.push(text_)`), so `x.text[0]` is the first text segment of the header. The purpose is to find which line the header appears on by matching its text against the lines. So we need this to work as a "contains" check, not as a regex search.

The simplest fix: escape all special regex characters in `x.text[0]` before using it in `new RegExp()`.

Let me now fix the code:

content.text:
No existing regex escaping utility. Let me check Node.js — there's no built-in `RegExp.escape`, but I'll need to implement it. In Node.js 22, there's `RegExp.escape()` but it's only available in certain versions. Let me just use a manual approach.

The fix should be in `addLinenos` function at line 12. I'll escape special regex characters in `x.text[0]` before using it in `new RegExp()`

content.text:
The third test ("given a file with headers embedded in code") fails! The issue is that `x.text` could be undefined — when a header has an opening tag captured via `onopentag` but the corresponding `ontext` fires with `text_` that gets pushed into the `text` array, but if the header closes without any text (or with empty text that gets pushed), then `x.text[0]` could be `undefined`.

Wait, looking at the error more carefully: `Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'replace')` — this means `string` is `undefined`. So `x.text[0]` is `undefined` for some header. Let me check the readme-with-code.md fixture

content.content:
1 README to test doctoc with edge-case headers. 2 3 4 5 ## Table of Contents 6 7 - [Single Backticks](#single-backticks) 8 - [Multiple Backticks](#multiple-backticks) 9 - [code tag](#code-tag) 10 - [pre tag](#pre-tag) 11 12 13 14 15 ## Single Backticks 16 `

not me single backticks

` 17 `## nor me single backticks` 18 19 ## Multiple Backticks 20 ``` 21

not me fenced

22 ## nor me fenced 23 not even me fenced 24 ------------------ 25 ``` 26 27 ## code tag 28

not me code

 29 ## nor me code 30 31 ## pre tag 32 
33	    
not me pre

34	    ## nor me pre
35	    not even me pre
36	    -------
37
38 Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

file.content:
README to test doctoc with edge-case headers. ## Table of Contents - [Single Backticks](#single-backticks) - [Multiple Backticks](#multiple-backticks) - [code tag](#code-tag) - [pre tag](#pre-tag) ## Single Backticks `

not me single backticks

` `## nor me single backticks` ## Multiple Backticks ```

not me fenced

## nor me fenced not even me fenced ------------------ ``` ## code tag

not me code

 ## nor me code ## pre tag 
    
not me pre

    ## nor me pre
    not even me pre
    -------

content.text:
The issue is that `` tags are matched by the `onopentag` as a tag, and they might not have text content. But wait, looking at the parser code more carefully: ```js if (name === "pre" || /h\d/.test(name)) { grabbing.push(name); } ``` `` doesn't match `/h\d/` and isn't `"pre"`, so it shouldn't be pushed onto `grabbing`. So the `` tag won't be a "header". But wait — looking at the headers that come from `

not me code

` — the `

` tag IS a header tag, but it should be skipped because the code tag handling... Hmm, let me re-read the parser. `code` is not in the `grabbing` condition. So `

` inside `` would still be pushed onto `grabbing`. But the `pre` tag handling explicitly skips text: ```js onopentag: function (name, attr) { if (grabbing[grabbing.length - 1] === "pre") return; if (name === "pre" || /h\d/.test(name)) { grabbing.push(name); } }, ``` So `` doesn't prevent `

` from being pushed. This means `

` inside `` IS treated as a header. But its text might be empty... Actually, looking at `

not me code

`, the text "not me code" would indeed become the text of `

`. So the text array shouldn't be empty. Let me think again... The error says `Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'replace')` which means `string` is `undefined` in `escapeRegExp` — so `x.text[0]` is `undefined`. This means there's a header with an empty text array. Wait, looking at the htmlparser2 behavior: when `

not me code

` is parsed, the parser sees: 1. Open `code` - not pushed to grabbing (not `pre` or `h\d`) 2. Open `h2` - pushed to grabbing 3. Text "not me code" - pushed to text array 4. Close `h2` - popped from grabbing, pushed header with text=[] ...wait, the `text` array is reset with `text = []` in `onclosetag`. So if there's a header with empty text, it would be `headers.push({ text: [], tag: ... })`, and then `x.text[0]` would be `undefined`. Hmm, but there IS text "not me code". Unless the parser fires `ontext` after `onclosetag`. Let me check: Actually, I think the issue might be that `htmlparser2` uses the `decodeEntities` option and the text could be split into multiple chunks. Let me investigate further. Actually, looking more carefully: the original code didn't have this issue with `new RegExp(x.text[0])` because `new RegExp(undefined)` returns `/undefined/` which is a valid regex (it matches the literal string "undefined"). But with my `escapeRegExp` function, `undefined.replace(...)` crashes. So I need to handle the case where `x.text[0]` might be undefined. Let me check if this was always happening or if it's a new issue... Actually wait — the original code at line 12 was: `if (new RegExp(x.text[0]).test(line))` — `new RegExp(undefined)` creates `/undefined/` which would test if the line contains the literal string "undefined". This is a pre-existing bug, but it didn't crash. My fix surfaces it. Let me look at what happens with the `

not me code

` case. The htmlparser2 Parser with `decodeEntities: true` — let me trace through what exactly happens:

input.description: Trace htmlparser2 behavior

content.text:
Interesting — `grabbing` is `[]` when `h2` opens because `code` wasn't pushed. But then `h2` IS pushed. So the text "not me code" gets associated with `h2`. And the header has `text: ["not me code"]`, which is fine. So the `readme-with-code.md` has 4 `

` headers inside code blocks (backticks and code/pre tags) that all get parsed. But let me check if markdown-to-ast prevents them from being included... Wait, the markdown parser extracts HTML blocks from the markdown. In the readme-with-code.md, the `` and `
` tags are markdown-level HTML blocks. When the markdown parser extracts them, they include the entire HTML including nested `
` tags. Then the htmlparser2 parses them.

But the backtick-wrapped ones like `` `
not me single backticks
` `` — in markdown, this is inline code, so the `
` inside backticks should not be interpreted as HTML. Let me check what happens with markdown-to-ast parsing

input.description: Check what markdown-to-ast extracts as HTML blocks

content.content: HTML blocks: []

input.description: Check what markdown-to-ast extracts from the code fixture

content.content:
HTML blocks: --- type: Html --- type: Html --- type: Html --- 
    
not me pre

    ## nor me pre
    not even me pre
    -------
type: Html

tool_use_result.stdout:
HTML blocks: --- type: Html --- type: Html --- type: Html --- 
    
not me pre

    ## nor me pre
    not even me pre
    -------
type: Html

content.text:
So the `` and `
` tags in the HTML blocks... Wait, the `
` block is the only one with actual `
` tags. Let me check:

The `
` block contains `
not me pre
`. But the `
` tag IS checked in `onopentag`:

```js
if (grabbing[grabbing.length - 1] === "pre") return;
```

So when we enter `
`, it gets pushed to grabbing. Then when `
` inside it opens, we check `grabbing[grabbing.length - 1] === "pre"` which is `true`, so we return early and don't push `h2`. Good, `
` inside `
` is skipped.

But `
not me code
` — does the markdown parser treat this as HTML or inline code? Looking at the output above, `...` is NOT in the HTML blocks. So markdown treats `` as inline and escapes it somehow? Let me check...

input.description: Check how markdown-to-ast parses code tag with h2

content.text:
Now I need to fix `escapeRegExp` to handle `undefined` or `null` values. But more importantly, the real fix should probably filter out headers with empty text arrays. Let me do both:

1. Make `escapeRegExp` handle undefined/null by returning an empty string
2. Or better, filter out headers with empty text in the `addLinenos` step

Let me do option 1 (making escapeRegExp handle undefined/null) since it's defensive and doesn't change behavior

content.content: The file /testbed/lib/get-html-headers.js has been updated successfully.
